channel.title: Projects on xpsong
channel.description: Recent content in Projects on xpsong

item.title: Data-driven approaches to understand human interactions with the environment
item.description:
People’s experiences of their surroundings can vary depending on where they are and their personal interests. Today, the internet and geo-located social media have opened up new ways for us to understand these differences. Technologies to interpret text and image content have also been improving, which can be used to assess the effects of the environment on people’s behaviour, sentiment and aesthetic experiences at specific places.

Many studies have assessed the accuracy of geo-located social media as a proxy for outdoor recreation, but fewer have examined how experiences vary according to different groups of people. With such bottom-up sources of data, it is also challenging to ensure proper data representation across different people groups. It is thus important to consider both the effects of ‘place’ and ‘people’ across multiple datasets, and to build integrated workflows that can help improve the reliability of these data-driven approaches.

item.title: The natural capital of city trees
item.description:
Over the past decades, there has been intensive effort to ‘green’ many cities around the world. Increasing the amount of tree cover has been particularly important, as it is associated with numerous benefits through the ‘ecosystem services’ they provide to city dwellers. For example, trees can lower temperatures by providing shade, mitigate air pollution by adsorbing pollutants, and reduce the risk of flooding by intercepting rainfall. Trees are thus a form of ‘natural capital’ that produce value for people.

Many cities have started to assess the status and health of these natural capital assets, which is an important step to incorporate the environment into holistic, national-scale planning. However, it is also crucial to weigh these benefits against the costs of tree planting and maintenance. There is also a need to consider the effect of time—specifically, how the growth and mortality of trees affect their associated benefits and costs as they age. A better understanding of these relationships will help us maximise the cost-effectiveness of trees in the long run.

item.title: Ecosystem services in urban landscapes
item.description:
Urban vegetation and green spaces provide cities with a number of benefits which are known as ‘ecosystem services’. These include reducing flood risk, cooling urban areas, and providing spaces for recreation.

Most research on urban ecosystem services has been conducted in temperate Europe and North America, but we know relatively little about how tropical urban areas can be designed to enhance ecosystem service provision. Tropical areas, particularly Southeast Asia, are rapidly becoming more urban, with cities growing larger and people living in higher densities. It is therefore important to better understand how urban greenery can contribute to enhancing the sustainability of such cities.

The main aim of this project is to use new types of data and modelling approaches to improve urban landscape design and obtain the greatest value from ecosystem services. Physical, ecological and socio-cultural parameters are used as inputs to model various types of ecosystem services. The landscape is designed using 3D point cloud data, which produces a representation of the landscape that is highly accurate. The iterative process of model simulation and design feedback provides opportunities to improve designs based on scientific results.

item.title: Feasibility of urban farming in a compact city
item.description:
Food security is emerging as a serious threat in many parts of the world. In cities, common strategies to improve the resilience of food supplies are to diversify the sources of food, and to optimise local production. In compact cities where land is scarce, vertical and rooftop farming have been explored as an alternative to conventional agriculture. However, the success of such measures rely on their environmental, social and economic feasilibility within the local context.

My Honors Thesis assessed the suitability of light conditions along the vertical surfaces of buildings in Singapore, a dense and compact city in the tropics. These conditions were compared against the light requirements of several vegetable crops that require high amounts of light, and that form a significant proportion of the staple diet in many Asian countries. Plant physiological variables such as dark respiration (Rd), light compensation point, light saturation point, photosynthetic capacity (Amax) and quantum yield (Φ) were derived. These were used to calculate plant light requirements, or the ‘Daily Light Integral’, which ranged from 10–30 mol m-2 day-1. Depending on the building height, orientation and configuration, light conditions along vertical surfaces ranged from 2–35 mol m-2 day-1.
